speaker: interviewer
text: Gisela Konopka, tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot say how long I was in those boxes. And, in fact, time is always difficult to determine. I will also go a little faster because I think there is so much to tell about all this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I want people to know this is 1936. And when we are put into a wagon to be brought to Fuhlsbuttel it's filled with men where the blood is caked on their faces. And I'm not making that up. It's just running everywhere. And they just sit there. They are already beaten like crazy. We get into the concentration-- yeah? Were they people you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody of these people. Oh, when I was in the cellar, there were people brought in that I knew, but we did as if we never had seen each other, yes. One I will name-- Helmut Kalbitzer, K-A-L-B-I-T-Z-E-R, was not a Jew and went later, once more, a long time into prison under the Nazis, and who later became one of the major political representatives of Germany after the war. So there. But otherwise, no, I didn't recognize anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the concentration camp, something strange happened. The SS-- is that clear? That's the black guard. Not black in the-- I mean the ones that wore the black uniform. Is very tall, definitely looks like a degenerate-- you can see that leer in that face-- very little.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he starts yelling. I mean, words you don't even want to repeat. You know, dirty Jew pig and whatever, all the time. And again, it was interesting, because I can't take very well people yelling at me. I had heard my father too often yelling. But at that moment, I thought I'm wearing a raincoat, and that's just running down. But he was yelling. But then he said something that made me feel very good. He yelled at somebody else, 'Don't bring in any more. The place is getting over-filled.' And I said, well, that means an awful lot of people are standing up here because this is not yet rounding up only Jews. This is rounding up resistors. And, oddly enough, I saw women. Oh, I thought they were old. Maybe they were 40. But that they were especially poorly treated by this guy. And I found out they were Jehovah's Witnesses, because they didn't say heil Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I get in my cell, there is a knife. Now, that's very unusual that I have a knife lying there, a sharp knife. But I knew immediately what they wanted is that I cut my arms, you know, so that they didn't have to do it. And I thought no, you do the dirty work if you want to. And that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in isolation, and I was about four or five weeks. I can't say exactly. I have it somewhere written down. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I will name a few things about the torture of being in isolation and not being allowed to do anything and being interrogated all the time and always telling them that I knew from nothing, which is about the only thing you could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of questions were they asking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, whom I knew. They had some leaflets. Who had written that? They knew. They knew. They knew a lot. I can explain how they had it, but I will not go into that right now. It's too much. I think what I want to say is I have-- always when I talk, especially with younger people, I always say I have still seen people hunted to death. And you must remember that. It's not having a gun and just shooting somebody, which is bad enough. But I was in my cell, and I was not allowed to get out. But I heard noises, and I could look through a little hole. There was a man, and it's winter, and the guards were standing around, and they made him run. Run. Throw yourself down on the ground. Get up. Run. This went on and on. They got hoarse and couldn't say it anymore. They got another bunch in to do the same thing until that man suddenly fell down. The blood spurted out of his mouth and his ears. And I still remember the hands coming up like, you know, like a bird, and he was dead. Hunting to death, and laughing while they're doing it-- this is something one has to remember, that people can be so cruel to another human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the first time you had seen somebody killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The first time-- but in that form, it especially--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: How can you feel about that? You say, this is the way the world will end. And people have asked me, did you think you will ever come out? No, I didn't think I would ever come out. I thought a lot of the people were somewhere else. I heard the man on top-- I mean, that was in the cell above me-- walking back and forth. I wrote a song, which I have, about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But how did I feel? That it's terrible that human beings can do this. And at the same time all of us-- all of us-- yeah, I remember once I thought what a pity you aren't ever getting out, because you wished you could tell people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to myself, but if I ever get out, I'll never, ever stop fighting that, ever. And I haven't. I know that because it's not something I imagine now, because I wrote it down much later once. It's-- I-- I-- The words were in my mind, [GERMAN]. I will be there to tell about that. See, I have never understood, frankly, when people tell me about they had been in this horrible concentration camp, and when they came to the United States, they never talked about it. I wouldn't. Both Paul and I-- and he has gone through horrible other things. Both Paul and I always talked about it. We didn't always have the opportunity, but whenever we had, we talked about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Getting back to being in isolation--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- only once, I will say, was I allowed to go out and get a shower. And a woman in front of me had-- I could see that both hands were broken. And later I heard that she was another anti-Nazi fighter. And they broke her hands, and she was always kept on her bed alone, but I don't know that. I just know it from story. But I saw they had broken hands. And when we had to leave back, they said we had to take a pail of water and bring it back. And I got so angry, thinking that she-- with those hands, she couldn't carry that. So I was angry, so I said I'll take her pail. And there was one woman who was a supervisor or whatever you want to-- a jailer. And she said OK, you can do it, but to the end. And I still remember that this was one of the highlights of my being in solitary. And I have taught that my students. Allowing people to do something for others is the greatest thing. It's better than any other stuff. It's something you learn in this moment. OK. Isolation-- suddenly, somebody comes and says, get out. And the door opens. Next to me was a big cell full of women. I think most of them were prostitutes, because they were just mistreated too, and they saw me, that I would be leaving alone. And I have felt human kindness. They rushed up to me, put their arms around me. If they kill you, we think of you-- I mean, that kind of thing. And these are people I had never known, never will know again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And back-- back to the place where we were arrested, and there's that man who always interviewed me, who had told me I would be there for the 1,000-year reich, which they always said, and who said, you can go. And I said, what? And he said, get going. Auf Wiedersehen. I never forget that. And I said, 'Not to you.' [LAUGHS] You know, 'Auf Wiedersehen' means 'I'll see you again.' And when I was out, I still remember I could not believe I was out. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to my so-called room. It was only five weeks, and it was as if somebody was always catching me. You don't believe that you are out. Now, very quick. At that moment, you are useless to any underground work because they have hurt you. They know you. There's nothing you can do anymore. That's about the worst. You feel like a useless human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I decided I have to go back to my mother, who lived still in Berlin, who was desperate. She was alone because my older sister, meanwhile, had gone to Israel. My younger sister was in London. My father had died. I don't think I said that. My father died already in the second year of the Nazi-- there's another story. But he died in a hospital from a heart attack because it was-- but I decided the best thing is to go to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father died of a heart attack because it was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were such horrible things, like the crystal night. I don't know whether they smashed in our windows or not. I don't know, but it was all around. Also, there was a neighbor whom my father had saved when the Nazis were not yet in power and tried to beat him up because he had said something bad, so my father had called the police and had helped him. That neighbor wouldn't talk to him anymore and practically spit on him. My father was, with all his anger and all that, also very sensitive. He had a heart condition, but that was too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his children-- he had come and visited me in Hamburg. Now, these are things I didn't tell all the detail. And he had met Paul, whom he did not want because he was not a Jew, you know? And, in that respect, I felt Jews were just as prejudiced as anybody else. And he didn't want me to be with Paul.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was already Nazi time, and my-- just the first year. And Paul showed him around, and my father loved him. It's a strange thing that I was told when he died, he was calling for both me and Paul. So in spite of that, people can also learn to forget prejudices. But I didn't tell that because I can't tell everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back to your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to my mother in Berlin. And my main point was that I have to get her out of the country. She was alone. There was no point anymore. And this is rather strange. Here I am, out of a concentration camp, going to all these bureaucracies-- they are all Nazi bureaucracies-- talking about that the store has to be sold. Sold-- I mean, it wasn't sold because they would take it anyhow. But that my mother has to go. I got everything organized for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I always said, what difference does it make? If they recognize me, I'm gone anyhow. So it didn't make any difference anymore. There is a strange thing. When you feel death is close anyhow, it doesn't make any difference. There are quite a lot of things that are interesting about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother put things together. You could still ship out some things at that time, but very little, and then we couldn't take any money with her and stuff like that. But we had friends, my sister's husband relatives, who said they would meet her. I forgot. I still think it was Leipzig, but I might be wrong. And then they would take her in a car and then go over to Israel, you know. And so I went with my mother on the train up to Leipzig and said goodbye. And then I was alone, totally alone. And I went back to-- there was still the store. Nobody had yet done anything, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think you were going to see your mother again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't think I would see anybody again. I didn't think I would see Paul again. He, meanwhile, had to get going, and he was in the worst situation in France. There are many other stories that I could tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after leaving your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: But at-- Yeah. I went back to that, and I saw Hilda Monte. Hilda Meisler was one of my younger sister's friends and also one of the children in my group. One of the most courageous women you can ever imagine. And she came to see me, and we talked with each other, and she went later to England. And she was later killed on the border of Austria and Switzerland when she was bringing again underground papers there. So these are all horrible stories. But I had one cousin in Berlin, and I want to say her name-- Lola Caminer, C-A-M-I-N-E-R. And I always wanted that one would put something, speak up for her, because nobody anymore would touch me-- I mean, even people who know me-- because I was dangerous. And Lola said, you come to my house. You stay with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to Lola. She had two children. I saw again how fear was absolutely overwhelming. The children couldn't play in the streets. They were called names. The child played with the ball. The ball went into a window. The police came and treated them as if they are criminals, you know? And this is still-- when is this? This is I think 1937 or '36 or '37, something like that. How did I get out of Germany? Very strange. I met a woman in the street, whom I knew, who was Jewish, young, and she looked very beautiful and suntanned. And I said, where have you been? And she says, oh, I was in Czechoslovakia, in one of those places where you get a lot of sun and bass and all that stuff. And I said, how did you go? Because I happened to know that she was-- oh, I forgot to say something, and I have to say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazis had already taken our citizenship away from us, because my parents originally were Austrians, and then we were naturalized in the 1920s. And I-- we certainly were born in Berlin, but we were naturalized citizens. So all our citizenship was taken, so we didn't have a passport. We had a passport. It was called a stateless passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew that about her too. And I said, how did you do it? She says, there is a doctor at the Czech [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] embassy, and he will help you to get permission. And in all my life, and I can name them over and over, there were people who help. It's never that they are cruelty. They are wonderful people. To do that very quick, I went there. He said, why do you have to go? I say, I feel bad. He said, that's not good enough to go to Carlsbad. Where did you go? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Czech embassy.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin. In the Czech embassy in Berlin, and I don't know his name. He was a physician. And he said you don't have to go because you feel bad. And I said, oh, I don't know what I had. I-- I invented something. My kidneys don't work, I think. And he said, that's right. That's-- we came to-- then he just wrote out something that I had to go to Carlsbad, which was the place for base to recover. I had to do something absolutely horrible. To go there, I had to get a stamp in that stateless passport in Hamburg. Now, there were still people of the underground that came to see me occasionally, and they said you go, not you go. We help you over the border. I said, I won't do it because there are too many people still that are connected to me. If I disappear illegally, they'll be pulled in. Can't do it. If I go, I have to go legally. There's no other way. I had to do one thing in Berlin for the underground, which was very hard. I had to bring some papers that were illegal papers, and I don't know what it was, to somebody else. I don't remember who that was. And I thought, I cannot take the concentration camp a second time. So we are not all heroes. But I did it, and it worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: But to go back, I decided I'm going back to Hamburg. Were these papers you had to bring to this person outside of Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was inside Berlin, but to another place in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was off limits to you for some reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I went back to Hamburg, and nobody will believe it. I always say thank God nobody is almighty because here was the-- the-- what do you call it? The bureau, the office, where they had taken me first [INAUDIBLE]. And here was the office where I went in with my passport and with the permission to go to Carlsbad. They gave me the stamp right away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is-- this is not real. I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] have to say that I didn't like to do all this because I felt from now on, I will not be able to do anything anymore for what we call the cause. But that isn't true, but we will see about that. A person came to me, and I'm feeling very sad that I forget his last name. It's Julius, J-U-L-I-U-S. You can't believe it. Julius too was Jewish, part of the ISK underground, was already hunted so that he couldn't anymore stay any night, that same night, the next night overnight. He had to always stay somewhere else, but he did it. He was a teacher. I once asked him when I met him, again at that time-- I said, Julius, you are much nicer than you were when I knew you way back before the Nazis. And I never forget when he said, 'I learned something. I learned that people need each other.' And he wrote some beautiful-- I have it in my book-- to his parents, you know, about how he has had to do what he had to do. But, anyhow, Julius came, and he said, you go on a train. I want to be-- and bringing you to that train so that we know that you are really leaving the country, that you are on that train. And, you know, again, I have to tell you that each time when I left, I left everything behind. There isn't much you take with you. But I have to say something. It's only last year that I was back in Berlin. I have been back in Berlin before, but under other circumstance. When I passed through the Potsdamer Platz, which is one of the major-- I suddenly remembered something I had never remembered before, and that is that there were the women with the violets, that sell violets, and Julius bought a bunch of violets and said something good has to be taken out of this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, all right. Very quick. I get into Czechoslovakia. By train?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, in Czechoslovakia are people who have helped refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- Where did you go in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Carlsbad. But I knew I would have to go to Bratislava because that's where a lawyer was who helped a lot of refugees. But I had to first go to Carlsbad. And I can't tell you-- I really don't know where I stayed. I know that I had no money and that I lived on the warm water that spurts everywhere in Carlsbad. So maybe some bread-- I don't know, but that's all I had. And I visited once a family. Mother had given me that address. Jews. And it was so normal. They were having a new baby. They were happy. It scared me. They didn't want to believe anything. There was an-- oh, now I know. In Carlsbad was a newspaper man who was against the Nazis. And that's the man-- that's-- he and his wife, whom I met-- they understood. They showed me that all the material of what went on in Germany was available.

speaker: interviewee
text: So nobody can ever tell me that they were not allowed to know. It was in the newspapers. Former refugees, there were books that they had published. People don't want to know, and that includes everybody. Nevermind whether they're Jews or not Jews. They don't want to know. What year was this that you went to Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's either '37 or '38. But they just didn't want to know. And it was scary to see that. Then I got to Bratislava, and the lawyer was-- and I don't know his name, and I can never thank him. The point was that the ISK and the underground needed every person. We thought we were very few, and we were very few, though there were many more that we didn't know about. But they wanted that I get a regular passport and that I get into Austria and start working against the Nazis again. And I have to say it wasn't something I looked forward to. I really didn't. But I felt so much the obligation because we all knew that they were terrorists. As I say, it didn't start with Auschwitz. I had friends, Jews and non-Jews, who were in Buchenwald-- B-U-C-H-E-N-W-A-L-D. I haven't seen it, but people came out from there, and they would tell me that this man-- and I forgot his name-- that he had to shovel his grave, and they put him in the grave, and they closed it. Now, we knew that, so we thought we have to continue. And so to get an Austrian passport, I had to marry somebody who was Austrian. This is something I don't like to talk about because it wasn't a marriage that had any meaning to me, but that I felt it as a pain. You know, it's not something I like. I-- that nice lawyer said something good to me. He said, well, in the olden emperors' times, the kings and queens had to marry somebody they didn't care about so that they can be kings and queens. Nowadays, it's the anti-Nazi underground people who have to do that. It was a nice way to say it, but it was terrible because you had to pretend, and I never liked to pretend.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this marriage come to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I say, there was another very dedicated man. He was in Paris. And they asked him, since he was an Austrian, whether he would go back to Czechoslovakia and then Austria and marry this woman just for the paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was through the ISK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And Paul was already in Paris, and we didn't like the idea at all. But what could we do?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you kept in touch with Paul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. That's a mistake to think because that's impossible. You couldn't write. There was no way of keeping in touch. Once in a while, through the underground, you would have somebody who could then say a word. We were separated for eight years during all that period and hardly knew from each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bratislava. There's another person who had to go through the same thing as I. Her name was Erna, E-R-N-A. And then we both-- I don't know whether we went at the same time. I think she went earlier. But as a happy bride with an Austrian passport, I had to go to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this person who married you? How did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's-- what do you call that? In the-- in the city hall. Somebody in the city hall.
